source_paper_title: Improving Envy Freeness up to Any Good Guarantees Through Rainbow Cycle Number

context:
Page 5, end of Section 1.2 (immediately after the discussion of \(R(2)\le 2\)); reiterated on page 16 right after Theorem 6 in Section 5.

We suspect that \(R(d)\in O(d)\). We believe that finding better upper bounds on \(R(d)\) is a natural combinatorial question, and better upper bounds to \(R(d)\) imply the existence of better relaxations of EFX allocations. Therefore, investigating better upper bounds on the rainbow cycle number is of interest in its own right, and we leave this as an interesting problem.
title: Determine the asymptotically tight rainbow cycle number in bounded-partite digraphs
background_latex:
Fix an integer \(d\ge 1\). A directed \(k\)-partite graph is a digraph \(G=(V,E)\) whose vertex set is partitioned into \(k\) (nonempty) parts \(V_1,\dots,V_k\), and edges may go between any ordered pair of vertices (including across parts), with no restriction on directions.

Consider directed \(k\)-partite graphs \(G=(\bigcup_{i=1}^k V_i,E)\) satisfying the following properties:

1) (Bounded part size) \(|V_i|\le d\) for all \(i\in\{1,\dots,k\}\).

2) (Pairwise in-neighborhood condition) For any two distinct parts \(V_i\) and \(V_j\), every vertex \(v\in V_i\) has an incoming edge from some vertex in \(V_j\), and every vertex \(w\in V_j\) has an incoming edge from some vertex in \(V_i\). Equivalently, for all distinct \(i\neq j\) and every \(v\in V_i\), there exists \(u\in V_j\) with \((u,v)\in E\).

3) (No rainbow cycle) There is no directed cycle \(C\) in \(G\) that intersects each part at most once, i.e., \(|C\cap V_i|\le 1\) for every \(i\in\{1,\dots,k\}\).

Define the rainbow cycle number \(R(d)\) to be the largest integer \(k\) for which there exists a directed \(k\)-partite graph satisfying properties 1)–3) above.
problem_definition_latex: Determine asymptotically tight upper bounds on \(R(d)\) as a function of \(d\). In particular, decide whether \(R(d)=O(d)\) holds (equivalently, whether there exists a constant \(C\) such that \(R(d)\le C d\) for all \(d\ge 1\)).
latex:
Fix an integer \(d\ge 1\). A directed \(k\)-partite graph is a digraph \(G=(V,E)\) whose vertex set is partitioned into \(k\) (nonempty) parts \(V_1,\dots,V_k\), and edges may go between any ordered pair of vertices (including across parts), with no restriction on directions.

Consider directed \(k\)-partite graphs \(G=(\bigcup_{i=1}^k V_i,E)\) satisfying the following properties:

1) (Bounded part size) \(|V_i|\le d\) for all \(i\in\{1,\dots,k\}\).

2) (Pairwise in-neighborhood condition) For any two distinct parts \(V_i\) and \(V_j\), every vertex \(v\in V_i\) has an incoming edge from some vertex in \(V_j\), and every vertex \(w\in V_j\) has an incoming edge from some vertex in \(V_i\). Equivalently, for all distinct \(i\neq j\) and every \(v\in V_i\), there exists \(u\in V_j\) with \((u,v)\in E\).

3) (No rainbow cycle) There is no directed cycle \(C\) in \(G\) that intersects each part at most once, i.e., \(|C\cap V_i|\le 1\) for every \(i\in\{1,\dots,k\}\).

Define the rainbow cycle number \(R(d)\) to be the largest integer \(k\) for which there exists a directed \(k\)-partite graph satisfying properties 1)–3) above.

Determine asymptotically tight upper bounds on \(R(d)\) as a function of \(d\). In particular, decide whether \(R(d)=O(d)\) holds (equivalently, whether there exists a constant \(C\) such that \(R(d)\le C d\) for all \(d\ge 1\)).
location_in_paper: Page 5, end of Section 1.2 (immediately after the discussion of \(R(2)\le 2\)); reiterated on page 16 right after Theorem 6 in Section 5.
open_problem_quote: We suspect that \(R(d)\in O(d)\). We believe that finding better upper bounds on \(R(d)\) is a natural combinatorial question, and better upper bounds to \(R(d)\) imply the existence of better relaxations of EFX allocations. Therefore, investigating better upper bounds on the rainbow cycle number is of interest in its own right, and we leave this as an interesting problem.
keywords: envy freeness up to any good
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The object is a finite digraph with bounded part size \(d\) and a simple-to-check forbidden-structure constraint (no directed cycle using at most one vertex per part) plus a local in-neighborhood condition. For small \(d\), one can brute-force or use SAT/ILP/CP-SAT to search for constructions with many parts, providing improved lower bounds or ruling out large \(k\) by infeasibility.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate solutions are explicit digraph constructions parameterized by \(d\) and \(k\); fitness can be computed automatically by checking the in-neighborhood condition and detecting a forbidden rainbow cycle (or returning a violating cycle). An evolutionary loop can explore families of constructions (e.g., algebraic, Cayley/shift-based, blow-ups) to push lower bounds on \(R(d)\) and to generate structural hypotheses that could guide proofs toward \(O(d)\) or sharper bounds.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The rainbow cycle number \(R(d)\), introduced by Chaudhury--Garg--Mehlhorn--Mehta--Misra, asks for the largest number of parts \(k\) in a directed \(k\)-partite graph with part sizes at most \(d\) and the strong pairwise in-neighborhood condition, under the prohibition of a directed cycle that uses at most one vertex from each part. The source paper proves the first explicit polynomial upper bound \(R(d)\le d^4+d\) via a constructive extremal argument: from \(k>d^4+d\) parts one can algorithmically build a cycle that visits each part at most once by first creating a cycle that may revisit a special part \(\widetilde V\) and then "bypassing" repeated visits through carefully selected distinct intermediate parts. They also give a linear lower bound \(R(d)\ge d\) via an explicit construction, leaving a wide gap and motivating the conjecture \(R(d)=O(d)\).

Subsequent work (as already noted in the source paper's acknowledgments and references) indicates that the \(d^4\) bound is not tight and can be improved by refining the bypass construction to only the consecutive pairs actually used in the final cycle, suggesting \(O(d^3)\)-type bounds. The EC'23 paper by Akrami, Alon, Chaudhury, Garg, Mehlhorn, and Mehta explicitly builds on the rainbow-cycle-number framework and reports an (almost) optimal guarantee for the fair-division application, which strongly suggests that the extremal bound on \(R(d)\) (or the effective parameter controlling the algorithm) is substantially sharpened beyond \(d^4\). However, based on the information provided here (no forward-citation analyses and no full metadata for the later papers), there is not enough to certify an asymptotically tight determination of \(R(d)\) itself, nor to confirm the conjectured linear upper bound.

At present, the state of the art grounded in the source paper is: \(d \le R(d) \le d^4+d\), with credible indications of improvements (possibly \(O(d^3)\) or better) in follow-up work, but no definitive resolution of whether \(R(d)=O(d)\) holds. The most promising directions are (i) tightening the bypass/representative-set method to reduce the exponent, and (ii) seeking structural characterizations of extremal examples (those with no rainbow cycle) that might force near-linear bounds, potentially via expansion-type arguments or refined Ramsey-avoidance analyses tailored to the strong in-neighborhood condition.

sources.title: EFX: A simpler approach and an (almost) optimal guarantee via rainbow cycle number
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces a simplified framework for the rainbow-cycle-number approach to partial EFX, and (per the title/abstract) obtains an (almost) optimal guarantee for the number of unallocated goods by improving the underlying extremal bound on the rainbow cycle number beyond the original polynomial bound.
sources.connection: Directly targets improved upper bounds on the rainbow cycle number (or closely related extremal parameter) that control the sublinear-unallocated-goods guarantee; any improvement corresponds to a better asymptotic upper bound on \(R(d)\).

sources.title: Fixed-point cycles and approximate EFX allocations
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops a cycle-based/fixed-point method for approximate EFX allocations, providing structural tools for finding improving cycles in auxiliary graphs that encode envy/championship relations.
sources.connection: While not phrased in terms of \(R(d)\), it contributes techniques for extracting short improving cycles in directed structures, conceptually adjacent to finding rainbow cycles in bounded-partite digraphs.

sources.title: Improving Envy Freeness up to Any Good Guarantees Through Rainbow Cycle Number

sources.theorem_or_result: Defines the rainbow cycle number \(R(d)\) for bounded-partite digraphs with the pairwise in-neighborhood condition and proves \(R(d)\le d^4+d\) (with a constructive cycle-finding argument). Also gives a lower bound \(R(d)\ge d\) and conjectures \(R(d)=O(d)\).
sources.connection: This is the originating source of the open problem; it establishes the first polynomial upper bound and frames the conjectured linear bound \(R(d)=O(d)\).

sources.title: Color-coding
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the color-coding technique for finding simple paths/cycles of length \(k\) in time \(2^{O(k)}\,\mathrm{poly}(n)\), a foundational tool for cycle-finding in large graphs.
sources.connection: Provides general algorithmic machinery for finding cycles with distinct colors/parts, relevant to the constructive aspect of finding a rainbow cycle, though the rainbow-cycle-number extremal bound is the main obstacle here.

sources.title: Graph Theory
sources.theorem_or_result: Standard reference for Ramsey theory and extremal graph theory background used to argue finiteness of \(R(d)\) via multicolor Ramsey numbers.
sources.connection: The source paper uses Ramsey-type arguments (via configurations of bipartite digraphs) to show \(R(d)\) is finite; Diestel is a canonical background reference.
